US Rowing

Quick Facts
  • Lauren is the daughter of Dr. Brian Reedy, M.D., and Sherry Reedy, an R.N
  • She is the youngest of five siblings (Nicki, Ben, Emily and Troy)
  • Lauren began diving at the age of 14 and also played ice hockey and softball growing up
  • Because she started the sport late, she says basics and drill work have never been a strong suit for her and spent the extra hours focusing on that in 2016
  • When it came time to be recruited, Lauren was still relatively unknown (she never qualified for a junior nationals)
  • Mizzou coach James Sweeney found out on the same day that two of the top divers he was recruiting had decided to go to other schools
  • Sweeney happened to be talking Legacy Diving coach Buck Smith, Reedy’s club coach, about how he wanted to find someone no one had heard of and make her successful
  • Smith told him about Lauren, and she ended up signing with Missouri, where she became an NCAA all-American
  • Even though she describes herself as a morning person who will sometimes wake up at 5 a.m. to enjoy the sun rise and stillness of the morning, she says the hardest part about her training is Monday mornings
  • When she is not diving, Lauren enjoys reading, biking, hiking, boating, sand volleyball and watching the sun rise.
  • Lauren majored in nutritional sciences and pre-med at Missouri
  • Now trains in North Carolina
Career Highlights
  • 2017 national champion (mixed synchronized 3-meter)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2017 – 10th (3m Springboard Synchro - Mixed)
  • Years of participation: 3m Springboard Synchro - Mixed 2017
  • Top finish: 10th – 2017 (3m Springboard Synchro - Mixed)

Quick Facts

  • Daughter of 1980 Olympic speedskating bronze medalist Beth (Heiden) Reid, and the niece of Eric Heiden, five-time Olympic gold medalist in speedskating at the Olympic Winter Games Lake Placid 1980

  • She was an NCAA champion cross-country skier at the University of Colorado before taking up biathlon in 2015

  • She has an undergraduate degree in applied mathematics and a graduate degree in engineering from the University of Colorado

  • Hobbies include mountain biking, climbing, rollerblading and microbrewery touring
Biographical Information 

Joanne Reid is the daughter of 1980 Olympic speedskating bronze medalist Beth (Heiden) Reid and the niece of Eric Heiden, the only Winter Olympian to win five gold medals in a single Olympics, which he accomplished at the Olympic Winter Games Lake Placid 1980.

Joanne Reid was an NCAA cross-country skiing champion at the University of Colorado before taking up biathlon in 2015. She made her IBU World Championship debut for Team USA in 2017 and, a year later, her Olympic debut at the Olympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018. 

Off the snow, Reid enjoys mountain biking, climbing, rollerblading and microbrewery touring, and says the number one thing she misses when she is on the road is Colorado craft beer.

Olympic Experience
  • 3-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, 18th (4 x 6km Relay - Women), 68th (15km Individual - Women), 72th (7.5km Sprint - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 11th (4 x 6km Relay - Women), 29th (10km Pursuit - Women), 34th (7.5km Sprint - Women), 57th (15km Individual - Women)
    • Olympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, 13th (4 x 6km Relay - Women), 15th (2 x 6 + 2 x 7.5km Relay - Mixed), 22th (15km Individual - Women), 86th (7.5km Sprint - Women)
World Championships Experience

  • Years of participation: 15km Individual - Women 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2023; 4 x 6km Relay - Mixed 2023; 4 x 6km Relay - Women 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2023; 7.5km Sprint - Women 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2023; 10km Pursuit - Women 2017, 2019, 2021; 4 x 7.5km Relay - Mixed 2021; 12.5km Mass Start - Women 2019
  • Top finish: 9th – 2019 (4 x 6km Relay - Women)

More
Although Reinprecht's life-long vision of becoming part of the U.S. Olympic Roster has come to fruition, her sight has not always been the clearest. As a one year old, the Perkasie, Pa. native had a cataract removed from her left eye resulting in her vision coming predominately from her right eye. Reinprecht joined the U.S. Women's National Team in 2010 alongside her sister Katie. Not only do both sisters compete for the U.S. National team, but they competed together for Princeton University as well.

bio.education: Clear Creek HS (League City, Texas) ‘02 Texas State University ‘06, Accounting
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Requist proved to be a quick-study in the sport of Nordic skiing. With only two years of experience, she has already earned two world cup podiums
  • Requist may appear humble and reserved, but she has a fiercely competitive and fearless side. On the anniversary of the cliff-jumping accident that left her paralyzed two and a half years ago, Requist commemorates the date by jumping even further - she goes skydiving
  • Requist has been an athletic and adventurous person her whole life. Before her injury, Requist worked as a flight attendant and stayed active snowboarding, biking and running
  • Within weeks of leaving the hospital, she was competing in handcycling races and contacting the National Sports Center for the Disabled to find out how she could get into a sit-ski.
Career Highlights
  • 2014: Paralympic Winter Games, 16th 12km, 19th 1km sprint
  • 2014: U.S. Paralympics Nordic Skiing National Championships, 2nd 6k biathlon, 3rd 10km, 4th 1km and 5km
  • 2013: World Cup Cable, Wis., 3rd 15km, 3rd 1km, 5th 10km
